title: On the BSD conjecture for certain families of abelian varieties with rational torsion
speaker: Emmanuel Lecouturier
abstract: Let N and p greater than or equal to 5 be primes such that p divides N−1. In his landmark paper on the Eisenstein ideal, Mazur proved the p-part of the BSD conjecture for the p-Eisenstein quotient J(p) of J0(N) over Q. Using recent results and techniques of the work of Venkatesh and Sharifi on the Sharifi conjecture, we prove unconditionally a weak form of the BSD conjecture for J(p) over a quadratic field K (which can be real or imaginary). This includes results in positive analytic rank, as the analytic rank of J(p) over K can be greater than or equal to 2 for well-chosen K.

proof_stages.label: BSD invariants and rank-zero formula
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews the analytic and algebraic invariants of a modular elliptic curve or abelian variety over Q and states the rank and rank-zero formula parts of the BSD conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Weak p-Selmer BSD at reducible primes
proof_stages.summary: Existing p-part BSD theorems rely on residual irreducibility, so the speaker replaces Mordell-Weil and Sha separately by the p-Selmer group and formulates the weak prediction that p-divisibility of the algebraic L-value is equivalent to nonzero p-Selmer, while emphasizing that this does not construct rational points.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein model X0(11) at p=5
proof_stages.summary: The main reducible case is set up using E=X0(11) and p=5, where rational 5-torsion is explained by Mazur's Eisenstein congruence between the cusp form f and an Eisenstein series modulo 5.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic-twist theorem and class-unit criteria
proof_stages.summary: For quadratic twists E_d with root number +1, Mazur's imaginary-quadratic result is recalled and the real-quadratic theorem is stated: 5 always divides the algebraic L-value, and divisibility by 25 is equivalent to nontrivial 5-Selmer and to an explicit class-number/fundamental-unit congruence.

proof_stages.label: Modular-symbol reduction of L-value divisibility
proof_stages.summary: The analytic half of the equivalence is reduced to congruences for modular symbols, motivated by the Eisenstein congruence f≡E mod 5 and made concrete through formulas expressing twisted L-values as sums of quadratic characters times modular symbols.

proof_stages.label: Mod-25 modular-symbol formula via K-theory
proof_stages.summary: To detect divisibility by 25 in the real case, a refined congruence expresses even modular symbols modulo 25 as a logarithmic term plus five times a homomorphism evaluated on K_2 Steinberg symbols built from continued-fraction convergents.

proof_stages.label: Level raising and Sharifi control
proof_stages.summary: Passing to level 11d replaces the continued-fraction expression by Manin-symbol data, and Sharifi's modular-symbol/K-theory results with level control convert the K-theoretic expression into the cyclotomic-unit calculation yielding the fundamental-unit condition.

examples.name: Untwisted five-part BSD check for X0(11)
examples.note: The speaker computes L_alg(E)=1/5, c_11=5, E(Q)_tors≅Z/5Z, and Mazur's vanishing of the 5-part of Sha to illustrate how the rank-zero BSD formula balances before studying twists.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the X0(11) BSD-at-5 ingredients—L-value 1/5, Tamagawa number 5, torsion Z/5, trivial 5-primary Sha—and checks the formula 1/5 = 5/5^2.

passes.reason: X0(11) is treated as the trivial untwisted base member of the quadratic-twist family and is also the minimal-conductor elliptic curve, used to verify rather than refute BSD at 5.
the untwisted d=1 case is the base member of the twist family and X0(11) is the minimal-conductor elliptic curve, used as a positive BSD-at-5 check.
X0(11) is the minimal-conductor modular elliptic curve used as a baseline BSD verification at p=5, not to refute a claim.

It is the trivial untwisted base member of the quadratic-twist family used as a direct BSD-at-5 check, not as a disproof.
X0(11) is the minimal-conductor elliptic curve used here as a positive BSD-at-5 check, not as a failure case.
The untwisted X0(11) is the trivial base member of the twist family and a minimal-conductor elliptic curve, used to verify the 5-part BSD formula rather than refute it.

X0(11) is used as a baseline verification of the 5-part BSD formula, not to refute a claim, and it is extremal as the minimal-conductor elliptic curve/modular curve example over Q.
X0(11) is used as a positive base computation of the 5-part BSD formula, and as the least-conductor elliptic curve over Q it is structurally extremal rather than adversarial.
X0(11) is used as a base verification of the 5-primary BSD formula, and is structurally extremal as the smallest-conductor/first genus-one modular elliptic curve.

examples.name: Heegner points as the usual source of rational points
examples.note: In response to a question, Heegner points are mentioned as an illustrative contrast: the Selmer nontriviality result does not by itself construct rational points when standard point-producing methods fail.
examples.engagement_reason: Heegner points are only invoked in discussion as the usual way to produce rational points, with no construction or computation carried out.

passes.reason: Heegner points are systematic CM special points with extra endomorphism symmetry, invoked as a constructive source of rational points rather than as a counterexample.
Heegner points are invoked as the standard construction of rational points, not as an adversarial example or a sporadic/degenerate/extremal object.
Heegner points are CM/special points with extra endomorphism symmetry and are cited as a standard construction of rational points, not as a counterexample.
Heegner points form a systematic CM construction invoked as the usual way to produce rational points, not as an obstruction or counterexample.
